company.name: Freeport-McMoRan
company.description: Freeport-McMoRan mines copper, gold, molybdenum, and other metals through large-scale operations in North America, South America, and Indonesia.

overview.title: Business
paragraphs: Freeport-McMoRan is a large international metals producer centered on copper, with meaningful gold and molybdenum exposure from long-lived mining assets.

Its portfolio includes the Grasberg minerals district in Indonesia, the Morenci district in Arizona, Cerro Verde in Peru, other copper mines in the Americas, and dedicated molybdenum mines in Colorado.

overview.title: Commodity Exposure
paragraphs: Copper is the core strategic product because electrification, grids, data centers, construction, and industrial equipment all rely on high-conductivity metal supply.
Molybdenum is smaller but strategically relevant because it is used in steel and specialty alloys, catalysts, lubricants, pigments, and other chemical applications.

moatNarrative: Freeport-McMoRan's moat comes from scarce mineral reserves, permits, geology, processing know-how, capital intensity, and operating scale rather than from consumer brand or software lock-in.

The moat is strong because large copper deposits are difficult to discover, permit, finance, build, and operate. It is not absolute because the output is commodity metal priced in global markets and partly substitutable through recycling, thrift, and new supply.
decentralizationNarrative: Primary copper and molybdenum mining is inherently hard to decentralize because ore bodies, concentrators, smelters, tailings systems, water rights, and environmental controls require large fixed assets.

The more credible decentralization pressure sits downstream: higher scrap recovery, local materials processing, open foundry tooling, transparent material provenance, and cooperative recycling networks that reduce marginal dependence on new mine output.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 126-150.

moat.rationale: Large, long-lived copper and molybdenum assets, including Grasberg, Morenci, Cerro Verde, and Colorado molybdenum mines, create high barriers through geology, permitting, capital intensity, processing infrastructure, and operating expertise.

decentralizability.rationale: Primary mine production is location-bound and capital intensive, but copper and molybdenum scrap recovery, open foundry tooling, and local materials processing can decentralize part of the downstream supply chain.

profitability.rationale: Freeport-McMoRan reported net income attributable to common stock of about $1.9 billion in 2024 and operating cash flows of about $7.2 billion, though commodity price exposure keeps profitability cyclical.

peRatio.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported a trailing P/E ratio of about 32.8 for Freeport-McMoRan as of May 2026; market-data pages move daily, so this is a point-in-time estimate.

marketCap.rationale: StockAnalysis reported Freeport-McMoRan market capitalization of about $89.11 billion as of May 22, 2026.

products.name: Copper
products.summary: Copper is Freeport-McMoRan's core product and is sold into electrical, construction, industrial, transportation, and infrastructure markets.
products.whyItMatters: Copper is a bottleneck material for electrification, grid expansion, data centers, motors, wiring, renewable energy, and industrial equipment.
replacementSketch: A realistic replacement path is not a direct substitute for primary copper, but a layered reduction of dependence on new mined supply through higher scrap capture, local remelting, design-for-reuse, and more transparent material flows.

Open hardware foundry tools and cooperative scrap networks could make small-scale recovery and casting more practical, while large industrial users still rely on certified refined copper for high-spec electrical applications.

alternatives.name: Open Source Ecology Induction Furnace
alternatives.summary: Open Source Ecology documents an open induction furnace concept for melting electrically conductive metals as part of a broader open hardware foundry stack.

openness.rationale: The project is documented as open hardware on the Open Source Ecology wiki, though maturity and completeness vary by subsystem.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Local metal melting and casting directly support distributed materials processing, especially for scrap streams and repair parts.

readiness.rationale: The concept is documented and plausible for makers or workshops, but it is not a drop-in replacement for certified industrial copper refining or large-scale mine output.

costLeverage.rationale: Using local scrap can reduce material and logistics costs in some repair and casting contexts, but quality control and energy costs limit broad substitution.

disruptionConcepts.name: Cooperative Copper Urban Mining
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated network of local scrap collectors, electricians, demolition contractors, repair shops, and small foundries could recover copper from buildings, motors, wiring, electronics, and industrial equipment, then route it into certified regional processors or lower-spec local casting loops.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: If copper demand rises faster than primary mines can respond, higher-trust local recovery networks can pressure miners by capturing more end-of-life metal and shortening the path from scrap to usable feedstock.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization mechanism is cooperative materials coordination rather than Bitcoin. Shared registries, open grading rules, and interoperable local processors reduce dependence on a few large centralized scrap buyers.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Participants publish available scrap lots, grade claims, location, contamination notes, and chain-of-custody records into a shared marketplace; processors bid for lots or issue standing prices by grade.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Cheating is constrained through weight tickets, photo evidence, random assay sampling, reputation histories, processor dispute records, and independent material certificates for higher-value lots.
failureModes: Copper purity and contamination risk can make local processing unsuitable for high-spec electrical uses.
Scrap theft, false grade claims, and collusive pricing can undermine trust unless verification is strong.
Recycling cannot fully replace new mine supply while electrification demand is growing.
adoptionPath: Start with transparent local scrap aggregation for demolition, electrical contractors, and repair shops.
Add assay-backed grading, processor reputation, and cooperative ownership for regional recovery hubs.
Connect proven hubs to manufacturers that can design products around certified recycled copper inputs.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model distributes collection, sorting, and some processing across local operators rather than centralizing all supply in primary mines.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Scrap markets already coordinate buyers and sellers, but stronger shared grading and verification would be needed for a more open cooperative model.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Collection and recycling are already established, while open local processing and trusted certification are the harder parts.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Recycling can reduce marginal demand for primary copper but is unlikely to eliminate the need for large mines during a demand growth cycle.

products.name: Molybdenum
products.summary: Molybdenum is produced by Freeport-McMoRan from dedicated mines and as a byproduct of copper operations, serving steel, alloy, catalyst, lubricant, pigment, and chemical markets.
products.whyItMatters: Molybdenum improves strength, hardness, corrosion resistance, and high-temperature performance in alloys and is also used in important chemical and catalyst applications.
replacementSketch: The credible open replacement path is not replacing molybdenum chemistry itself, but recovering more molybdenum from catalysts, ferrous scrap, and superalloy scrap.
A more open recovery loop would combine better tracking of molybdenum-bearing materials, shared processing recipes where safe, and regional processors that can aggregate specialized waste streams.

disruptionConcepts.name: Molybdenum-Bearing Scrap Recovery Network
disruptionConcepts.summary: A specialized recovery network could identify molybdenum-bearing catalysts, ferrous scrap, and superalloy scrap earlier in the waste stream, aggregate them transparently, and route them to qualified hydrometallurgical or metallurgical processors.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Better coordination around molybdenum-bearing waste can reduce dependence on primary molybdenum production by making dispersed catalyst and alloy scrap easier to find, verify, price, and process.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is a shared coordination layer for small holders and processors, not a token-first design. If payments or escrow are needed, open payment rails could help, but the core mechanism is materials traceability and processor interoperability.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Refiners, machine shops, chemical plants, scrap yards, and alloy users register eligible molybdenum-bearing materials with grade estimates, origin, hazardous-handling requirements, and pickup or processing terms.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Trust depends on chain-of-custody records, safety documentation for spent catalysts, processor audits, assay sampling, hazardous-waste compliance records, and penalties for false declarations.
failureModes: Spent catalysts may be hazardous and require regulated handling that small local operators cannot provide.
Low volumes and mixed alloys can make recovery economics unattractive outside dense industrial regions.
Open coordination does not remove the need for qualified processors and environmental controls.
adoptionPath: Map local sources of molybdenum-bearing catalysts, ferrous scrap, and superalloy scrap.
Create standardized intake records, assay workflows, and processor directories for compliant recovery.
Pool volumes through cooperative or municipal-industrial hubs until processors can bid efficiently.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model decentralizes identification and aggregation of molybdenum-bearing scrap, while actual refining remains specialized.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The materials are identifiable and recyclable, but hazardous catalysts and mixed alloy streams require careful compliance and verification.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Recovery routes exist, but practical deployment depends on local industrial density, assay capacity, and regulated handling.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Recycling can matter at the margin, but Freeport's primary and byproduct molybdenum position remains protected by mine scale and established industrial demand.
